coredata.dc:title: Electrochemically enhanced peroxidase-like activity of nanohybrids for rapid and sensitive detection of H2O2 and Dopamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Sherazee, Masoomeh
Ahmed, Syed Rahin
Das, Poushali
Srinivasan, Seshasai
Rajabzadeh, Amin Reza
coredata.dc:description: Longer detection times and low sensitivities are the two main drawbacks of applying colorimetric detection methods in field applications where rapid and sensitive detection is required. A discovery of the enhancement of nanozymatic activity via electrochemical means which significantly reduces the analyte detection time and enables ultra-low detection levels has been reported in this paper. A novel, simple and facile approach for the synthesis of carbon quantum dot-gold nanoparticles (CQD-AuNPs) has been developed. The CQD-AuNPs showcased catalytic activity through the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and followed the characteristic Michaelis-Menten equation. Surprisingly, the catalytic activity of CQD-AuNPs nanohybrids enhanced considerably while the reaction was completed under an electrochemical process and assisted the detection of H2O2 and Dopamine (DA) with a limit of detection (LOD) value of 240 nM, and 0.75 μM, respectively. Most importantly, the detection time decreased by 60-fold compared to conventional nanozymatic reactions. The work covered in this paper provides a novel approach to develop the nanocomposite and a new detection technique that can be utilized across various fields of science and engineering to improve and exploit enzymatically dependent reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Nanozyme
H2O2
Dopamine
Colorimetric assay
Electrochemical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetic assessment of TMB and H2O2
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs, AuNPs and CQD-AuNPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the support from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada in the form of Discovery Grants to ARR and SS ( RGPIN-2019-07246 and RGPIN-2022-04988 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs (inset: image of solution under UV light); (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs; (c) XRD data for CQDs; (d) TEM image of CQDs-Au NPs and (e) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs-Au NPs and (f) XRD data for CQD-AuNPs.

Results of H2O2 detection: (a) Calibration Curve for H2O2 in the linear range of 0 – 1 mM; (b) Calibration Curve for H2O2 in the linear range of 4 – 80 mM; (c) Calibration Curve for H2O2 in the linear range of 0.1 – 1 mM and (d) Calibration Curve for H2O2 using electrochemical process in the linear range of 0.4 – 2 µM (inset: the image of solution color).
Terephthalic acid (TA) test results: (a) Formation and reaction of hydroxyl radical with terephthalic acid & (b) Fluorescence spectra of the reaction product.

(a) Absorbance spectra, after reacting for 30 min, depicting the blue peak at 650 nm for varying concentrations of DA (b) Calibration Curve for Dopamine in the linear range of 10 – 75; (c) Calibration Curve for Dopamine in the linear range of 1 – 8 using electrochemical process in PBS buffer and (d) Calibration Curve for Dopamine in the linear range of 1 – 8 using electrochemical process in simulated blood (inset: the image of solution color).
Schematic presentation of the proposed sensing method.
Comparison study of LOD values for present work with others reported for H2O2 detection.
Comparison study of K m and V max values for present work with others reported using H2O2 as the substrate.
Comparison study of K m and V max values for present work with others reported using TMB as the substrate.
Comparison study of LOD values for present work with others reported for DA detection.

head.ce:title: Electrochemically enhanced peroxidase-like activity of nanohybrids for rapid and sensitive detection of H2O2 and Dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West, Hamilton, ON L8S 4L7, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biomedical Engineering, McMaster University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West Hamilton, Ontario, Canada L8S 4L7

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering Practice and Technology, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West, Hamilton, ON L8S 4L7, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering Practice and Technology, McMaster University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering Practice and Technology, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West Hamilton, Ontario, Canada L8S 4L7

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Biomedical Engineering, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West, Hamilton, ON L8S 4L7, Canada.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biomedical Engineering, McMaster University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Longer detection times and low sensitivities are the two main drawbacks of applying colorimetric detection methods in field applications where rapid and sensitive detection is required. A discovery of the enhancement of nanozymatic activity via electrochemical means which significantly reduces the analyte detection time and enables ultra-low detection levels has been reported in this paper. A novel, simple and facile approach for the synthesis of carbon quantum dot-gold nanoparticles (CQD-AuNPs) has been developed. The CQD-AuNPs showcased catalytic activity through the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and followed the characteristic Michaelis-Menten equation. Surprisingly, the catalytic activity of CQD-AuNPs nanohybrids enhanced considerably while the reaction was completed under an electrochemical process and assisted the detection of H2O2 and Dopamine (DA) with a limit of detection (LOD) value of 240 nM, and 0.75 μM, respectively. Most importantly, the detection time decreased by 60-fold compared to conventional nanozymatic reactions. The work covered in this paper provides a novel approach to develop the nanocomposite and a new detection technique that can be utilized across various fields of science and engineering to improve and exploit enzymatically dependent reactions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQDs-AuNPs nanohybrids for nanozyme based detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are synthetic nano-scale enzymes that can be created to have specific, desired properties, most commonly: increased catalytic activity, higher stability, improved selectivity and specificity, lower cytotoxicity and greater cost efficiency. The ability to design this kind of reactive nanomaterial is preferred because it is a more reliable alternative to organic enzymes that are usually more expensive to harvest and store, as well as less predictable [1–12]. Established and proposed applications of nanozymes include biosensors, wound healing, antibacterial and disease therapies, anticancer therapy, environmental protection and electrochemical sensing among various [13–21]. Neurotransmitters (NT) are chemical messengers, sending signals from nerve cells to target cells. They help to regulate physiological conditions such as heartbeat, blood pressure, breathing, muscle movements, stress response, hormone regulation, digestion and healing [22]. Considering the importance of NT monitoring in the body, recently, numerous researchers have turned their focus to developing nanozyme-based NT detection.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) present wide applicability across industries and initiatives [6–8, 23–26]. CQDs have diameters of less than 10 nm, and are mainly composed of sp2 hybrid conjugated carbon as the core encased in a shell of organic functional groups or polymer aggregates [1]. CQDs, when compared to other graphitic materials, have greater biocompatibility, lower toxicity, are chemically inert and more cost-efficient regarding production. Their overall optical and definable fluorescence properties are favoured for biosensing [2]. Presently, there are several established techniques of CQD synthesis. To list a few; ultrasonic methods, hydrothermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, microwave-assisted synthesis, modified and Hummer's method [13–20]. The synthetic approaches for CQD production are commonly categorized into two methods: top-down and bottom-up. The former requires the breaking down of larger carbon structures while the latter generates CQDs from precursor materials such as citrate, carbohydrates and polymer–silica nanocomposites [2,8].

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have steadily gained traction over the years because of their many advantageous electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical and optical properties. AuNPs are small gold particles (diameters: 1–100 nm). Currently, there are several methods of synthesizing AuNPs. The most popular processes are chemical reduction and stabilization, electrochemical synthesis, production via microorganisms, plants and enzymes, and in the presence of ionic liquids [9]. Producing gold on the nanoscale level allows for greater exposed surface area, creating more space for elevated levels of reactivity, targeting and modification which increases functionality. AuNPs are less cytotoxic – compared to other metallic nanoparticles – making them a prime candidate for in vivo applications (e.g. drug and radiation therapy). Lastly, AuNPs can be easily tuned to continually adjust their properties [10–13]. The impressive biocompatibility of AuNPs permits its facile and stable conjugation to several organic and inorganic materials that can produce a large array of novel compound/nanohybridized materials that could exhibit synergistic effects in the desired application. Conjugation, synthesis methodology, protein structure, and adjoining particle morphology affect the overall activity of the AuNPs.

Given the extensive outreach of its precursor components, CQD-AuNPs, with their fluorescent and colorimetric properties, have immense utility across diverse fields and operations. Buk and team manufactured CQD-AuNPs to serve as an electrochemical, enzymatic biosensor [12]. The cost-efficiency and adaptability of CQDs in conjunction with the chemical inertness and electrochemical capabilities of AuNPs formed a highly selective biosensor that could reliably detect glucose in human serum. Recently, Ahmed and group applied a similar technique for the detection of thiocyanate (SCN-) in food samples using graphene quantum dots-AuNPs [14]. The AuNPs formed in the presence of SCN- were found to be smaller in size and this phenomenon increased the surface area available for reaction. Additionally, the hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging ability of SCN- also improved the nanozymatic activity, and consequently fluorescence quenching, of CQD-AuNPs.

Applying an external electric field can impact electronic interactions such as oxidation states in metal systems and modify thermodynamic and kinetic properties of elementary reactions by influencing the reaction energies and rate constants [27]. The movement of electrons augments the chemical reaction by encouraging the stabilization of ionic and charge transfer states thus forcing the reaction to take place more rapidly [28]. Combining these effects with the nanozymatic activity of the CQD-AuNPs is expected to exponentially increase the rapidity of the reaction.

Dopamine is a NT concerned with blood vessel function, kidney function, heart rate, mood and sleep. A shortage or surplus of dopamine could lead to schizophrenia, ADHD and obesity among several other health conditions [29]. Therefore, being able to monitor dopamine is crucial for observing the efficiency and efficacy of a treatment plan, as well as for diagnosing. The activity of NTs is rapid and short-lived, and so quick, reliable and easy detection should be the focus of any sensing system.

The detection of H2O2 is incredibly relevant to several biosensing applications because it is a molecule that is profoundly involved - as a reactant and a product - in numerous chemical reactions (including enzymatic) in living cells [30–32]. By creating sensors that are highly selective and specific to H2O2, the study of biological systems may be refined. Nanohybrids can be used as a catalyst in oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions, in addition to simultaneously playing other roles such as stabilizing and reducing agents because of their multifunctionality [33–35]. By confirming the presence and concentration of H2O2 in living cells, scientists will be able to study the health, efficiency and catalytic propensity of organic and inorganic agents in vivo and in vitro, thus confirming the immense usefulness of a nanohybrid for biosensing applications.

In this study, a new method to synthesize CQD-AuNP nanohybrids was presented that exhibited peroxidase-like enzymatic nature and enabled to quantify H2O2 and dopamine in simulated blood media. Remarkably, the nanozymatic activity of CQD-AuNPs nanohybrids under an electrochemical process had commendably increased and the detection time drastically decreased.

Hydroquinone powder, Triethanolamine solution, distilled water, Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4⋅3 H2O), 30% w/w hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium acetate buffer, TMB, Dopamine hydrochloride and Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich USA. All other chemicals used here are of analytical grade.

The CQDs were synthesized using a simple one-step approach. In brief, 50 mg of Hydroquinone powder was dissolved in 25 mL of dH2O. Next, 373 mg of triethanolamine was added to the Hydroquinone solution to achieve a 50 mM triethanolamine molarity. Double distilled water was added to the mixture solution to reach the 50 mL mark. The solution turned from colourless to vibrant yellow. Then, the solution-containing flask was transferred onto a hot plate at 100 °C and stirred for 60 min under the fume hood. After the reaction, the resultant product turned into a dark brown colour and was dissolved in 20 mL of double distilled water. Next, the solution was sonicated for 5 min and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 25 min followed by filtering using a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove large particles. Lastly, the dark brown solution was dialyzed for 24 h and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

Nanozymatic reaction was confirmed via colorimetric detection. To evaluate the nanozymatic activity 1:1 vol solution of 5 mM TMB and H2O2 was added to the CQD-AuNPs. In a 96-well microplate, 100 µL of CQD-AuNPs nanohybrid and 100 µL of TMB- H2O2 were added to a well. The solution was left to react for 30 min, after which a blue colour was expected. To get a more quantitative result, the UV absorbance spectral measurements were acquired. This was also the method used to evaluate the H2O2 detection abilities of the nanohybrid. All tests were carried out at room temperature (25 °C) in sodium acetate buffer solutions (pH 3.6 – 5.6) to best replicate the most probable conditions for rapid, field-testing scenarios.

The CQD concentration to prepare the CQD-AuNP hybrid was optimized to obtain superior properties. The optimizations of the CQDs were performed within a concentration range of 0.03125–1 mg mL−1 at room temperature. Starting at 1 mg mL−1 and halving the concentration until 0.03125 mg mL−1, the nanozymatic ability was verified by following the protocol under ‘Nanozymatic Activity’ listed above. Optimization was discerned by identifying which concentration of CQDs produced the deepest blue and highest peak at a wavelength of 650 nm.

The CQD-AuNP nanohybrid was prepared using a simple one-step approach. 500 µL aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (0.19 mM) was added to the 1000 µL aqueous solution of CQDs (0.5 mg mL−1). The resultant mixture solution of the CQD-AuNPs was kept at room temperature for 60 min to yield stable a purple-coloured solution. After completion of the reaction, the solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. Consequently, the CQDs-AuNP hybrid was collected and store at 4ᴏC for further analysis.

Conducted in a similar fashion to CQDs concentrations optimizations. 50 mM stock solution of H2O2 was prepared from 30% w/w H2O2. From there, appropriate volumes of the 50 mM H2O2 solution were added with sodium acetate buffer to yield 10 mL of varying concentrations of H2O2 to be assessed during the reaction. For TMB, 24 mg of TMB was dissolved in 5 mL of DMSO to yield a 20 mM stock solution, from which further samples of varying concentrations were procured through dilution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic assessment of TMB and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the kinetic ability and propensity of the TMB and H2O2 species to participate in the nanozymatic reaction, the effects on the absorbance values (at 650 nm) of varying concentrations of TMB and H2O2 over the course of 60 min, at 5 min intervals, were noted. TMB was tested within a concentration range of 0 – 20 mM during which the concentration of H2O2 was fixed at 0.05 mM. Also, various concentrations of H2O2 (0 – 24 mM) were tested while the TMB concentration was kept consistent at 10 mM. The values were then converted to initial velocities using a modified version of Beer-Lambert’s law. From there, the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk plots were constructed to determine the K m and V max kinetic parameters.

Dopamine detection was conducted similarly to the nanozymatic activity/ H2O2 detection tests through colorimetric detection. Briefly, a 1:1 vol solution of 10 mM TMB and 1 M H2O2 was mixed with the CQD-AuNPs and then varying concentrations of dopamine (0 – 100 µM) were added. Sensitivity was determined by the inhibition of the characteristic blue colour. In a 96-well microplate, 100 µL of CQD-AuNPs, 100 µL of TMB- H2O2 and 100 µL of DA were added to each well. The solution was left to react for 30 min, after that the absorbance values at 650 nm were recorded.

Another nanozymatic detection test that relied on yellow colorimetric enhancement was also used. For this method, 50 µL of 50 μM H2O2 and 50 µL 0.5 mg mL−1 CQD-AuNPs were added into wells of a 96-well plate before stirring in 50 µL of varying concentrations of dopamine solution (0 – 100 µM) into individual wells. The solution was left to react for 30 min, after that the absorbance values at 450 nm were recorded.

The ability to form hydroxyl (•OH) radicals in the presence of CQD-AuNPs (0.5 mg mL−1) was observed via the TA test. Firstly, 0.5 mM (100 µL) aqueous solution of TA was mixed with the CQD-AuNP solution (50 µL) and kept at room temperature for 10 min. Then, H2O2 solution (10 mM, 50 µL) was added to it and after 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction product was recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The incorporation of nanozymes into colorimetric techniques has unleashed a range of innovative and more efficient detection methods when compared to its natural enzyme predecessor. Largely, contemporary research articles in nanozymatic biosensor development contain two main selling points: the introduction of new nanozymatic materials and the ability to easily modify the material’s surface. The former includes examinations of their characteristics and potential applications, while the latter focuses on customizing them to reach desired levels. Studies of both factors have proven the importance of generating free radicals and/or scavenging them for the successful completion and elevation of nanozymatic activity. It has been established that free radicals are involved in the oxidation reaction of a chromogenic substrate in a peroxidase reaction, which consequently produces a colour change. To date, no one yet reported the way of faster oxidation reaction of a chromogenic substrate which might significantly reduce the detection time and enhance the detection sensitivity. The authors of this paper have endeavoured to apply a new electrochemical-based method to significantly augment this oxidation reaction – specifically with TMB as the substrate – to radically reduce detection time ( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs, AuNPs and CQD-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared through a simple heat treatment process as shown in Fig. S1. HRTEM was employed to analyze the morphology of CQDs. The CQDs ( Fig. 1a) were well dispersed with an average size range of 3–5 nm. Several studies were carried out to investigate the optical characteristics of CQDs. The aqueous solution of the CQDs showed a broad peak at 270 nm which was ascribed to the π –π * transition of the C═O bonds (Fig. 1b). The fluorescent spectra demonstrated the excitation-dependent emission behaviour of the CQDs. The emission signals of the CQDs were collected upon excitation from 310 to 450 nm (Fig. S2a). This photoluminescent behaviour is most likely due to particle size variation and the distribution of surface emissive trap sites [3,36]. The maximum emission peak of the CQDs was detected at 448 nm at 370 nm excitation wavelength with a Stokes shift of 78 nm. To evaluate the potential application of the CQDs, the stability of these nanoparticles was examined. The effect of storage time on the fluorescence signal of CQDs was investigated, but no substantial changes in the intensities were noticed as depicted in Fig. S2b. The FTIR studies were carried out to get insight into the CQDs' structural and functional groups. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2c) of the CQDs showed absorption bands at 3258 cm−1 ascribed to stretching vibrations of –OH and N–H bonds [37]. Peaks at 2915 cm−1, 2828 cm−1, 2361 cm−1, and 1573 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H, C–N, and in-plane bending of N–H [38,39]. The band at 1014–1075 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–O–C, and C–O groups [40]. The appearance of these oxygen-containing functional groups at the surface of the CQDs would aid in their dispersion in polar solvents including water, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, and PBS etc., which would be greatly useful for their potential biological applications. The broadened peak (at 26.7°) in X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the CQDs, indicates the amorphous structure of CQDs (Fig. 1c).

The TEM images of the CQDs and CQDs-AuNPs hybrids are displayed in Fig. 1a and Fig. 1d, respectively. Most likely, because of the attachment of CQDs to the surface of AuNPs, the surface of the hybrids appeared to have a rough texture. The CQDs-AuNPs hybrids were spherical in shape having sizes of about 25 nm. UV-Vis Spectroscopy was employed to measure the number of discrete wavelengths absorbed by the nanohybrid. The UV–visible absorption results (Fig. 1e) of the CQDs-AuNPs hybrids disclosed two distinct peaks at 270 nm and 550 nm, respectively, indicating the existence of CQDs and AuNPs [13]. As depicted in Fig. S3a, the aqueous solution of the CQDs was yellowish however the CQDs-AuNPs hybrids showed dark purple colour under daylight. Moreover, CQDs-AuNPs hybrids were well dispersed in an aqueous solution and were stable for a long time (approximately 1 month). XRD analysis was used to observe the crystalline structure and orientation of the CQD-AuNPs. In the XRD data (Fig. 1f), the peaks at 26.7°, 38.6°, 44.9°, 65.4° and 78.1° for CQDs-AuNPs, assigned to the diffraction from the (002), (111), (200), (040), (220) and (311) planes, respectively, of Au crystals, also corroborated the successful formation of CQDs-AuNPs.

The nanozymatic activity of the CQD-AuNPs was confirmed through the process outlined in Fig. S4a wherein the oxidation of TMB was observed in the presence of the nanohybrid and H2O2, which was evident through the onset of a bright blue colour. This peroxidase-based oxidation reaction is produced via the formation of ·OH radicals by the decomposition of H2O2 [41]. The CQD-AuNPs catalyze this reaction by supplying active sites for adsorption and interaction between TMB and H2O2. This is primarily the reason behind the samples with higher concentrations of CQD-AuNPs relaying a stronger blue colour than lesser concentrations within the same time frame while the conditions for TMB and H2O2 were kept identical (Fig. S4b). To reinforce this, the reactions were stopped by adding 10% sulfuric acid (H2SO4) which caused the colour to change from blue to yellow but the pattern of colorimetric intensity remained the same (Fig. S4b).

After the confirmation of the nanozymatic activity, the parameters were then optimized. The optimal concentration of CQDs in the CQD-AuNPs solution was found to be 0.5 mg mL−1 as Fig. S5 depicts it as facilitating the highest absorbance value at a wavelength of 650 nm, after 30 min, when concentrations of other parameters were kept static. Similarly, the optimal concentration of TMB was deemed as 10 mM because it also displayed the highest absorbance among an array of 0 – 10 mM in 2 mM intervals. 15 mM and 20 mM TMB were also tested for nanozymatic activity and while they did exhibit a stronger blue colour, successively, both concentrations also incurred significant crystallization and precipitation at the bottom of the well, indicating that 10 mM could stably portray the results of the oxidation reaction over the course of 45 min. In contrast, the smallest volume of H2O2 that could still result in a discernable blue colour was examined. Through experimentation this was found to be 0.05 mM (50 mM) ( Fig. 2a). The H2O2 data points were then used to construct two calibration curves, the first covering the linear range of 0 – 1 mM (Fig. 2b) and the second involving 4 – 80 mM (Fig. 2c) of H2O2. Interestingly, when conducting the overall reaction using the electrochemical procedure, the detection range can be minimized by several orders of magnitude. The Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) technique was used to improve nanozymatic activity at different concentrations of H2O2. A ramped voltage from − 1–1 v was applied at a scan rate of 0.1 V/S. Redox reactions require a substantial overpotential to create an adequate current to complete the process. At room temperature, this would take several minutes to hours for an enzyme at the nanoscale to meet this constraint. Hence, by externally supplying a potential and creating a current, the reaction reaches the necessitated potential within lesser time limits by several orders of magnitude. Based on the CVs shown in Fig S6, a substantial oxidation current from oxidation of TMB (in presence of CQD-AuNPs and H2O2) at an applied potentials of 0.21 V and 0.39 V resulted in discernible peaks. Here the influence of CQD-AuNPs were studied by electrochemical CVs as shown in the Fig. S6b. This means that the threshold of oxidation of TMB was dramatically hastened in the order of 30 s in the presence of CQD-AuNPs under an electrochemical setting, leading to enhanced sensitivity to the detection of H2O2. The mechanism and characteristics of the cyclic voltammetry results from the study are presented in Fig. S6a-d.

As shown in Fig. 2d, a linear detection range of 0.4–2 µM was achieved. Using this calibration curve, the LOD was computed to be 240 nM via the standard deviation method (LOD = 3.3 S/K). Where S is the standard deviation of the intercept divided by the square root of the number of samples and K is the slope. The R2 came to 0.9887, revealing a strong relationship between the two variables. The LOD is compared to other studies of nanozymatic detection for H2O2 in Table 1, indicating that the present sensing method is resoundingly more sensitive than the other reported articles.

The Michealis-Menten constant (K m) is the concentration of the substrate that allows the enzyme to reach half of its maximum velocity (V max). The lower the K m value, the stronger the affinity between the substrate and enzyme. The K m values for TMB and H2O2 were 5 mM and 2.34 mM, respectively. V max for TMB and H2O2 were 5 × 10−7 mM/sec and 4.096 × 10−3 mM/sec, respectively. Concentrations were contrived by manipulating Beer-Lambert’s Law: A = e
×
c
×
L, where e is the absorption coefficient of oxidized TMB and is a constant value (39 000 M−1 cm−1), L is the path length for the light to pass through (1 cm) and c is the concentration of substrate in the solution. The kinetic values of the substrates in relation to the nanozymatic activity of the CQD-AuNPs are compared to other nanocomposites in
Tables 2 and 3. The former compares the kinetic performance of various nanocomposites using H2O2 as the substrate and the latter uses TMB. The common features among all the materials are that they share some or all the compositional materials of CQD-AuNPs, observed substrates and/or target analytes. CQD-AuNPs kinetic activity is highly competitive when considering H2O2 as the substrate and fairly competitive with TMB as the substrate. This demonstrates that with added optimizations and functionalization, the nanohybrid is able to support rapid reactivity which is an especially attractive feature for biosensing-based practical applications.

Here, the hydroxyl radicals have a significant contribution to the nanozymatic activity of CQD-AuNPs. For example, hydroxyl radicals initiate the oxidation reaction of TMB which consequently produces the blue colour. To confirm the formation of hydroxyl radicals in the reaction media, a terephthalic acid test was performed. It is well-known that the reaction of terephthalic acid with hydroxyl radicals procures a fluorescent hydroxyl terephthalic acid product ( Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence peak of this hydroxyl terephthalic acid (located at 425 nm) was produced in the presence of CQD-AuNPs, TA and H2O2, however, no fluorescent peak was observed in the absence of H2O2. These results confirmed the formation of hydroxyl radicals in the reaction system.

Given the impressive ability of the CQD-AuNPs nanocomposite to detect H2O2 through electrochemical enhancement of its nanozymatic activity, the detection of dopamine was also explored for its potentially diverse applications downstream. Colorimetric detection assays were employed, and the sensitivity was determined by the resultant inhibition of the expected bright blue colour after 30 mins. By increasing the concentration of DA, the absorption peak at 650 nm decreased ( Fig. 4a) showcasing the inhibition of the catalytic activity of the CQDs-AuNPs. This colorimetric diminishment was caused by the usurpation of·OH by the DA resulting in fewer available for the oxidation of TMB which elicits the colour change [58]. Furthermore, a good linear relationship was obtained between the concentrations of DA (20 – 80 mM) and absorbance intensities as depicted in Fig. 4b using the conventional method. However, the detection range could be 10-fold lower when the reaction is implemented under the electrochemical process. As shown in Fig. 4c, a detection range of 1 – 8 mM was achieved through the proposed technique in PBS media. Moreover, a similar result was successfully obtained in a simulated blood sample thus reflecting the superiority of the proposed method in complex media. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.75 mM using values from the trendline of the above calibration curve as per LOD = 3.3 S/K. Where S is the standard deviation of the intercept divided by the square root of the number of samples and K is the slope. The R2 came to 0.9966 showing a strong relationship between the two variables. The LOD is compared to other contemporary works in Table 4. The electrochemical detection of DA was investigated using CV technique. The oxidation peaks of TMB (along with CQD-AuNPs and H2O2) consistently shifted to 0.11 V and 0.29 V in the presence of dopamine species in the electrolyte. This can be attributed to the fact that the dopamine-based inhibition of TMB + H2O2 + CQD-AuNPs requires less potential to complete redox reactions. This shift in oxidation potential is characteristic to a chemical compound species and can be further investigated for sensing applications. In this study, a similar hastening of TMB oxidation in the order of 30 s in the presence of CQD-AuNPs under an electrochemical setting indicating the detection of DA was observed. The mechanism and characteristics of the cyclic voltammetry results from the study are presented in Fig. S6a-d.

The second colorimetric detection test was used to compare the sensitivity of both methods using the newly formed CQD-AuNP nanohybrid. A greater concentration of dopamine was expected to portray a deeper yellow colour, which would accordingly display a characteristic peak at 450 nm. Typically, dopamine is converted into aminochrome by H2O2 in the presence of oxidase species causing the solution to turn from clear to yellow [59]. The nanohybrid was, again, implemented as a catalytic agent in this reaction. However, no such activity was observed as all concentrations of dopamine produced equally faint yellow colours and immensely weak peaks at 450 nm by the end of 30 min. So, while there was a minute distinction between the negative control (no dopamine) sample and samples with dopamine, there was no clear difference between the samples of dopamine, therefore, suggesting poor sensitivity of the second detection method.

In conclusion, a novel in-situ method for the fabrication of CQD-AuNP nanohybrids and the electrochemically sourced enhancement of its nanozymatic activity has been reported in the present work. The synthesis method has proven to be viable as characterization studies (HTREM, UV–vis spectroscopy and FTIR) depicted features of both CQDs and AuNPs. Nanozymatic studies showed that the material acted as a catalyst in the oxidation of TMB – mimicking the activity of a peroxidase enzyme which is frequently found in many natural biological systems. Kinetic profiles were developed, placing the CQD-AuNPs as a competitive nanozyme among its contemporaries. With further functionalization, the nanohybrid can adopt better affinity and ultra-rapidity. The applicability of the nanomaterial was observed through H2O2 and DA detection utilizing an electrochemical process. The consequent LOD for H2O2 and DA detection was recorded to be as low as 240 nM and 0.75 μM, respectively. The present fast and facile synthesis method of CQD-AuNP nanocomposites and their electrochemically increased nanozymatic activity technique can be advantageous in promoting better detection systems for in vitro, ex vivo and in vivo studies across a wide range of industries and shows significant promise in the fields of bioimaging and drug therapy.

Masoomeh Sherazee: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Syed Rahin Ahmed: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Poushali Das: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Seshasai Srinivasan: Supervision, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Amin Reza Rajabzadeh: Supervision, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the support from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada in the form of Discovery Grants to ARR and SS (RGPIN-2019-07246 and RGPIN-2022-04988).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.132576.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
